title: Working with APIs
summary: Build robust API clients with retry logic, caching, and rate limiting

sections.title: API Clients Are Like Restaurant Ordering
sections.content: An API client is like a waiter taking your order to the kitchen. You send a request (order), the kitchen processes it (server), and brings back a response (food). Professional API clients handle common issues: network failures (kitchen dropped the order — retry), rate limits (kitchen is too busy — wait), and caching (same customer orders the same dish — serve the pre-made one). A well-built client abstracts these concerns so the rest of your code just calls fetchData() and gets data.

sections.title: Fetch with retry and timeout
sections.content: Wraps fetch with exponential backoff retry and abort controller timeout

sections.title: Rate Limiting and API Contracts
sections.content: APIs enforce rate limits — maximum requests per minute. Respecting limits requires tracking request timestamps and queuing or delaying excess requests. Use a token bucket algorithm: you have N tokens, each request consumes one, and tokens refill at a fixed rate. Additionally, parse Retry-After headers from 429 responses. For REST APIs, follow the contract: use correct HTTP methods (GET for read, POST for create, PUT for update, DELETE for remove), handle pagination via Link headers, and cache ETags for conditional requests.

sections.title: Ignoring error status codes and edge cases
sections.content: The #1 mistake developers make is only handling the happy path (status 200). Wrong: const res = await fetch(url); const data = await res.json(); return data; — this crashes on 404, 500, or network errors. Correct: check res.ok, handle specific status codes (404 = not found, 429 = rate limit, 500 = server error), catch network errors, and implement retries with backoff. A production API client handles every HTTP status category appropriately.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Build an API client for a weather service. Implement: getCurrentWeather(city) that fetches and caches results in a Map for 5 minutes (use timestamps), getForecast(city, days) that handles pagination if the API returns partial results, and a rate limiter that allows max 10 requests per minute. Include proper error handling for 404 (city not found), 429 (rate limit), and network errors.
hints: How do you check if a cached entry is still valid based on its timestamp?
What headers or status codes indicate rate limiting from most APIs?
How do you track request timestamps to enforce 10 requests per minute?
